paragraphs.body: Almost every Sunday as a boy, my family and I would go to church. We would go with my dad's side of the family and attend the service. Afterward, we would go to my aunt's house for lunch. Her husband, Uncle Bill, would never attend church and would rarely attend family events. Every time, without fail, Uncle Bill would be on the back porch, rocking on his expensive wooden chair and looking at the "fat of the land" while puffing on his pipe, exhaling a somewhat soothing vanilla sent from his mouth with every puff. We always entered the house through the back porch for some reason; maybe it was the view, or maybe it was to greet Uncle Bill right as we arrived. My aunt, joined by my mom in the kitchen, would usually cook Turkey and mash with some biscuits or chicken Casserole, my Favorite. While lunch was being prepared, Uncle Bill, my dad, and I would all sit on the back porch, eyes locked on the cows, horses, sheep, or distant deer hiding out in the woods. I talked about their life on the farm growing up and how I "got it easy" and drilled the message of "living through god's word" into my head. These conversations on the back porch shaped how I speak and carried myself and gave me a sense of belonging; without these conversations on the back porch, I would be lost.

annotations.text: Uncle Bill would be on the back porch, rocking on his expensive wooden chair and looking at the "fat of the land" while puffing on his pipe, exhaling a somewhat soothing vanilla sent from his mouth with every puff.
annotations.comment: Great details and voice. Love this.

These conversations on the back porch shaped how I speak and carried myself and gave me a sense of belonging; without these conversations on the back porch, I would be lost.
annotations.comment: I'm super interested. What does it mean, to you, to be "lost"? What do you "belong" to? What has Uncle Bill, your family, your place, taught you? What are you proud of?

paragraphs.body: To live in the suburbs, Georgia means knowing
The gut-wrenching smell of cow manure while going down the road.

The hatred for mosquitos on a blazing humid summer night, The tiring aromatizing smell of Friend Chicken everywhere you go, The sound of Football coming from the TV every second, The food comatose that comes with Georgia folks' hospitality, The Sabbath day is the day of worship; My southern tongue is different than most people's. I do not have a Southern accent besides the occasional Southern-sounding word popping out. My southern tongue is presented through how I talk to people. It is the southern kindness that comes out of me when I speak. Making sure I use manners and respect the person I am having a conversation with. It comes out more when I am talking to people who are strangers, professors or teachers, and elders. This type of tongue was forced on me heavily when growing up by my parents getting onto me for not speaking to someone with respect or my Uncle Bill not talking to me at all. To this day, my Uncle Bill does not take any type of shit or disrespect from anybody. Anytime I gave him disrespect, he would show it right back, using ugly language or just not saying anything at all. For example, I texted my Uncle Bill to see if I could come over and sent a text saying, "Hey Bill, you got anything planned for today?". I sent a few other text messages after that, and none to which he responded. He was not respawning because I called him Bill instead of Uncle Bill. This is how many of my family members expected to be spoken to, which has molded how I speak to others. In a way, I always speak in my southern tongue, but it changes when I am with my friends. When I am with my friends, I can truly be myself and speak what my mind is telling me to say, whether it is mean, disrespectful, or inappropriate, because we understand each other. We often use silly words or phrases that we create when talking. Some of the words we use are beerdd, skibidi, and sus. Beerdd essentially means good shit, skibidi means stupid or something weird, and sus means someone, or something said was suspect. The word we use the most, and my favorite word is beerdd. It is my favorite lingo because it means so much more than a simple good job. When we use this word, we are reminded of the memories and moments when the word was created and when it has been used; no one besides us knows what it means, so on the surface, we look like a bunch of dumbasses, but in reality, it strengthens our connection with one another. Looking from an outsider's perspective, some of the words we use make absolutely no sense, which shows how creative we are when together. Whether it is endless conversation or non-stop antics, not a moment goes by where I don't enjoy their presence, and having a connection like that with a group of people makes me feel like I belong. I may not always feel that way, but when I am with my friends, there is no doubt in my mind.

annotations.text: "Hey Bill, you got anything planned for today?". I sent a few other text messages after that, and none to which he responded. He was not respawning because I called him Bill instead of Uncle Bill.

paragraphs.body: Going to a college over a thousand miles away has been challenging; I am completely separated from everything I have labeled "home" in my life. I feel that I cannot fully be myself or talk in a way that truly represents who I am. While this is the case now, hopefully, as I adapt to my new environment, a sense of belonging will come with it. During the move-in week, I met everybody on my dorm floor. When talking and exchanging names with everyone, it felt like they spoke a different language than I was. Most of my floor's people were from the Philly area, and I could not comprehend their lingo.

annotations.text: Going to a college over a thousand miles away has been challenging; I am completely separated from everything I have labeled "home" in my life.
annotations.comment: Is this also a new and different idea? How might it relate to Uncle Bill and/or your secret language? What is "home" to you? (not just physically but also emotionally)

paragraphs.body: Having a different language or tongue in which you speak is more than the words coming from your mouth; it is about the connections and people embedded within them. It's about finding who you are; having a language where you feel at home gives you a sense of self and security, knowing you will always have a come. For me, home is the savory smell of chicken casserole, the sweet smell of the tobacco pipe, and the southern hospitality of the people and families I was surrounded by. Wherever you are in life, pause and look around. Do you feel a sense of belonging in your community?

annotations.text: It's about finding who you are;
For me, home is the savory smell of chicken casserole, the sweet smell of the tobacco pipe, and the southern hospitality of the people and families I was surrounded by.
